“Hard to replicate” is industry baseline
Every photonics company claims “sub-micron precision.”
So do competitors like Lumentum Holdings, Coherent Corp., and Broadcom Inc..
Hard ≠ unique. If hyperscalers care, they won’t single-source a tiny cap such as $SIVE

Title: Utility Truck with Roof-Mounted Light Array — Photonics / LiDAR Plausibility Assessment
Timestamp: March 23, 2021 | Santa Barbara, CA (702)
Tags: #Photonics #LiDAR #UtilityVehicles #Optics #PerceptionVsReality #ForensicAnalysis
Forensic Category: Visual Assessment / Technology Plausibility

⸻

⚖️ Core Proposition

Not all high-intensity light arrays or mounted equipment indicate advanced sensing systems. However, certain configurations can overlap with technologies used in mapping, scanning, and illumination.

The task is to separate:
👉 What is commonly deployed
from
👉 What is theoretically adaptable

⸻

🔍 Observed in Image

Pickup truck with roof-mounted light bar + auxiliary lighting cluster
Trailer-mounted equipment consistent with a wood chipper / landscaping machinery
Residential driveway setting
No visibly distinct sensor housings (e.g., spinning LiDAR dome, multi-lens rig, stabilized gimbal)

⸻

🔁 Effect → Cause Analysis

Observed Effect:
Large, multi-module light array mounted above cab

Most Likely Cause (Common Use):

High-lumen LED light bars used for:
Night work visibility
Landscaping / construction operations
Off-road or utility illumination

👉 These systems can appear intense on camera due to:

Lens compression
Reflective surfaces
Sensor bloom

⸻

Secondary Consideration (Technically Plausible, but Not Evident Here):

LiDAR / photonic systems typically require:

Dedicated sensor units (often cylindrical or box modules)
Clear line-of-sight mounting
Stabilization or calibration hardware
Power + data integration (visible cabling or mounts)

👉 None of those are clearly visible in this image.

⸻

⚠️ Important Distinction

A light bar ≠ LiDAR system

However:

Some industrial vehicles can carry both lighting + sensing equipment
Advanced mapping rigs (surveying, autonomous testing) use:
LiDAR
RGB cameras
IR sensors
        —but these are visually distinct when present

⸻

🧠 Conclusion

👉 Based on visible evidence, this appears to be a standard utility/work truck with high-output lighting, not a dedicated photonic or LiDAR deployment system.

👉 While dual-use adaptation is theoretically possible in broader contexts, there is no clear visual confirmation of LiDAR or advanced sensing hardware in this instance.

⸻

📌 Provenance Note

Visual interpretation grounded in:

Known vehicle equipment configurations
Optical behavior of high-intensity LEDs
Standard LiDAR hardware characteristics

text:
Daily Recap: The historic run continues
- $QQQ 21.6% in 23 sessions followed by the $SPY 15.2% and the $IWM at 17%
- $XLK continues to be the market dominant group. $AAPL very near an all time high with the obvious horses being $INTC $AMD $MU. 
- Photonics work up once again after being quiet for a few weeks. $AAOI $CIEN $LITE $VIAV $LWLG $AXTI. The group is due up next for earnings this upcoming week. 
- Software $IGV is also giving the impression that we may have bottomed. Some names are already leading that group and on Thursday we had a great earnings reaction and follow through from $TWLO. $DOCN $FSLY $RNG $FIVN $BAND $NBIS ones to watch. 
- $XLE was still the leading group for the week and it's hard to write those names off when you look at the structure on many of those stocks. $CRAK $BOAT $OIH a few places to watch over the upcoming weeks. 
- $BTC $ETH stage 4B setups unfolding with $CRCL being the only real place of interest for me for now. 
- Memory names are being lead by 4 incredibly powerful trend that show no real signs of slowing. $SNDK $STX $MU $WDC. 
- We got through major earning reports last week and this week is also an important one. Focus on names gapping up on large volume and watch the for continuation setups. $MXL is a great example. 
- Keep universe lists updated and I will see you all on Monday!

text:
Memory and photonics have already run hard, but Layer 5 (Capacity Operators) has the biggest catch-up left. 🤖

$DGXX, sitting at only $240M micro-cap, looks like one of the cleanest pure-play #GPUaaS rare setups in the entire AI infrastructure stack.

Most peers are still years behind - stuck in permitting, transformer shortages, and grid upgrades.

DGXX has already completed the hard pivot: owns 70 MW approved power + commissioned ARMS 200 modular Blackwell pods at Alabama. This is literally the fastest way to turn power into revenue with scarce live Blackwell GPUaaS capacity.

Balance sheet is rock-solid: zero debt, ~$93M strong liquidity with no forced dilution. 

The stock has gone through classic micro-cap volatility. Now it sits at a clear inflection point.

First anchor GPUaaS deal signed - $19.6M SubQ AI revenue goes live May 15. This proves the model works and derisks everything. Advancing hyperscaler discussions with customers desperate for immediate capacity.

Superstar team with hyperscaler DNA:  Venkat Rangasamy (ex-Oracle AI Factories) as VP AI Infrastructure, @hansvestberg (ex-Verizon CEO) co-founding USDC, @GerardRotonda (ex-CFO Deutsche Bank) on the board. Talent this caliber only joins a microcap when they see something truly special.

The recent #USDC spinoff is brilliant and shareholder-friendly: with subsidiary entity raises dedicated capital for pods & GPUs separately - no dilution or debt for DGXX parent entity. DGXX keeps 55% ownership and the majority upside.

Roadmap with high visibility: 10 MW by Q3 2026 → ~$137–152M ARR + colocation → $11–$30/share (4-9x from here). Full 70 MW Alabama scaling → $7B–$12B+ market cap upside.

It’s not if but how fast - hyperscalers are hungry for immediately available power and capacity, and DGXX already has it. And, this is just the beginning of a multi-year AI capacity supercycle - demand for live power and GPUs will only keep growing.

$DGXX reminds me of the early-stage #IREN/#CIFR, both rerated 5-10x+ from pre-contract lows - the risk/reward looks exceptional from here, and it is still completely under the radar.

This could be us retail’s chance to get ahead before market finally connects the dots 🔥 (institutions flood in and analysts raise guidance.) 

Disclaimer: not financial advice. Always DYOR.

text:
The tools split because the physics spans 4-5 orders of magnitude in wavelength. An RF antenna is centimeters. A photonic waveguide is nanometers. 

Tackling both in one numerical simulation would be computationally nuts, so we got HFSS for RF and Lumerical for photonics.

Deep learning models can learn an embedding over EM fields that isn’t frequency-constrained.  We can tackle RF and photonics with the same model.

That’s actually what we’re building at @arenaphysica

text:
How is $KOPN weaved into all of this photonics, AI, datacenters talk?!

Kopin is a direct play on AI data center photonics / optical interconnects: 

Neural I/o repurposes Kopin’s proprietary MicroLED pixels (via their patented bi-directional NeuralDisplay architecture) as ultra-high-speed optical transceivers. It moves data using photons instead of electrons, replacing dense copper wiring and traditional laser-based systems between GPUs, processors, boards, and racks in AI/smart data centers.

This addresses the exact pain points (power, bandwidth, heat, scalability) that drive CPO demand. It promises the same (or better) performance with a fraction of the power per bit and eliminates expensive laser modules—positioning it as a disruptive, lower-power alternative/complement to the laser-heavy CPO stack ($SIVE’s InP DFB laser arrays, $POET’s interposers, etc.).

https://t.co/jZIG8z9CBJ has placed a $15M initial purchase order to fund the demonstration chipset. Kopin owns 19.9% of https://t.co/jZIG8z9CBJ and is the exclusive manufacturer of the Neural I/o chipsets. Kopin emphasizes its U.S.-based MicroLED manufacturing for secure supply chains (a big plus in AI/defense).

Stifel recently reinstated coverage with a Buy and $5.50 target; the stock has shown momentum on the news.

Kopin is entering the same optical interconnect layer for AI infrastructure with a novel MicroLED approach that sidesteps some of the traditional laser challenges. This reinforces another high-upside, U.S.-centric player in the photonics boom for AI data centers + defense. LONG $KOPN $SIVE

Kopin is a direct play on AI data center photonics / optical interconnects: Neural I/o repurposes Kopin’s proprietary MicroLED pixels (via their patented bi-directional NeuralDisplay architecture) as ultra-high-speed optical transceivers. It moves data using photons instead of electrons, replacing dense copper wiring and traditional laser-based systems between GPUs, processors, boards, and racks in AI/smart data centers. This addresses the exact pain points (power, bandwidth, heat, scalability) that drive CPO demand. It promises the same (or better) performance with a fraction of the power per bit and eliminates expensive laser modules—positioning it as a disruptive, lower-power alternative/complement to the laser-heavy CPO stack ($SIVE’s InP DFB laser arrays, $POET’s interposers, etc.). https://t.co/jZIG8z9CBJ has placed a $15M initial purchase order to fund the demonstration chipset. Kopin owns 19.9% of https://t.co/jZIG8z9CBJ and is the exclusive manufacturer of the Neural I/o chipsets. Kopin emphasizes its U.S.-based MicroLED manufacturing for secure supply chains (a big plus in AI/defense). Stifel recently reinstated coverage with a Buy and $5.50 target; the stock has shown momentum on the news. Kopin is entering the same optical interconnect layer for AI infrastructure with a novel MicroLED approach that sidesteps some of the traditional laser challenges. This reinforces another high-upside, U.S.-centric player in the photonics boom for AI data centers + defense. text:
Photonics, Optics & Memory stocks saw another massive breakout to new all time highs last week. 

Many of these stocks are up well over 100%+ year to date.

After the recent mag7 earnings reports; I think data centers/compute providers will be the next sector to run big.

$NBIS - $CRWV - $CIFR And $IREN

On the various Mag7 earnings calls, they noted their current bottleneck wasn’t the availability of chips, but the availability of compute & power/energy. 

There are smaller data center co’s like $SNLH $BITF $APLD - but the 4 I mentioned earlier are my favourites. 

Trade idea;
Wait for a breakout above previous highs for an entry, or a re-test of this descending triangle breakout.

text:
Biggest moves come from biggest stories.

- memory supercycle
- gov't stake in INTC
- photonics bottleneck
- war = big oil move
- software short (anthropic narrative)
+ plenty more

Some new(er) "stories" I'm watching:
- live commerce
- tokenization of assets
- new drone use-cases
- personalized health
- ai agents

Price *usually* shapes the narrative...but sometimes you can sniff out the future narratives before price even realizes what's happening.

I have followers across many industries, what are you guys sniffing out that nobody else realizes yet?

text:
PO, CPO를 포함한 Photonics 섹터에서
PIC 설계, 제조사들은 200G/lane이상을 이야기하며 그 이상을 바라보지만

제대로된 EIC 200G/lane을 설계하고
그걸 Foundry로 만들 수 있는 기업은 한정적인데 사람들은 그걸 간과하고 있는듯하다.

Photonics는 PIC, EIC, DSP, LASER, PD Detector, 차세대 패키징이 한번에 가능해야 비로소 제대로 만들어졌다 할 수 있다.

PIC 200G를 시연에 성공했어도, PD Detector가 그걸 못받아 들이는 사양이면 무용지물이고, EIC가 신호를 제대로 못주면 그것 또한 의미 없는 것이다.

그 신호를 밀어줄 EIC/driver, 받아줄 PD/TIA, 보정할 DSP, 버틸 laser, 그리고 그걸 낮은 loss와 높은 yield로 묶는 패키징이 같이 맞아야 한다.

결국 Photonics는 부품 기술이 아니라, 시스템 통합 싸움이다.

text:
$AMD just reported a monster ER and sent every CPU stock to the moon. Up +15% after hours.

Since I started publicly sharing my portfolio I’ve been clear about where I’m putting my money and why:

The AI buildout: 

Photonics / Memory / CPUs / NeoClouds

So why CPUs and why now?

$AMD said it best on the call: in the past the CPU-to-GPU ratio was 1-to-4 or 1-to-8. Now it’s moving toward 1-to-1. And with agentic AI, you could end up with more CPUs than GPUs. That’s a structural shift.

Here’s how my CPU basket is doing since I entered:

$INTC (January) – entry at ~$55, bought the local top because I’m the worst trader ever. Now +105%.

$AMD (late March) – entry at ~$202, bought the local bottom because I’m the best trader ever. Now +76%.

$ARM (late March) –entry at $137 after the CPU for AI thesis started gaining traction. Now +62%.

If you think you’re late I can assure you you’re not. Not super early either. But agentic 

AI spending is just getting started and CPUs are in the critical path of every dollar spent.

Still bullish all CPU plays.

In a new blog, Joseph Chon explains why integrating MACsec directly into coherent DSPs inside ZR/ZR+ pluggable modules represents a meaningful shift in how scale-across networks are secured. Compared to embedding MACsec in switch ASICs or placing dedicated security devices on circuit boards, module-based MACsec reduces cost, complexity and deployment risk while making upgrades significantly easier. Marvell has already introduced several industry firsts enabling this approach, including the Electra 2nm 1.6T coherent DSP, the Libra 800G coherent DSP and the Aquila M coherent-lite DSP, all with integrated MACsec. By 2030, pluggables are projected to account for approximately 85% of scale-across ports shipped. text:
Threadguy explains why he slammed Zcash this weekend 

“Everybody in the stock market is so rich right now. Everybody is up so much money… there is just trillions of dollars that have been printed in the stock market”

“They’re ripping everything. We’re doing photonics now, everything’s an AI bottleneck… anything that could be remotely related to AI that hasn’t pumped, rip that, South Korea stocks now… rip that”

“When I saw the metals to full AI to South Korean stock rotation I’m like people are desperate for some reflexivity”

“There’s three assets in crypto that just haven’t moved Hyperliquid, Zcash and Bitcoin. Zcash is like an $8B coin… it’s worth nothing”

“I’m watching Google add $500B in a day and I’m saying to myself man they’re gonna pop it”

“Bitcoin, Zcash and Hyperliquid all of a sudden look good. I was bidding Zcash a little bit but then I just slammed over the weekend”

“Bitcoin, Zcash, Hyperliquid are the three set up… I just got the tingle”

text:
Photonics and memory are both getting re-rated right now because AI infrastructure got declared a national security priority. 

That changes the capital flow entirely. Government tailwind plus hyperscaler spend plus the CPO transition hitting simultaneously.

Optimistically you’re looking at 40-60% CAGR on photonics through 2028 with 

CPO as the accelerant. Memory, specifically HBM, is already running 50%+ growth and that probably holds or steepens as cluster density increases.

The Goldman re-rate just gave institutional cover for what was already obvious to anyone close to the hardware stack.

text:
I’ll only say this once.
Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang just revealed the ‘Five Layer’ AI Model.
These 5 layers include;
1. Energy - $BE $OKLO $VRT
2. Compute - $NBIS $IREN $CIFR
3. Photonics - $AAOI $LITE $SIVE
4. Memory - $MU $SNDK $STX
5. Chips - $NVDA $AMD $AVGO

All of these stocks will explode in 2026 & 2027 as AI continued to expand.
Tell me, which stock are you most bullish on?

text:
What if computation left no evidence it happened?

Not encryption. Not anonymization.

Physically. Zero. Trace.

🧵

#security #hardware #photonics #cryptocurrency

Huge beat on $QCOM could be signaling the bottom of the Smartphone Market, where it makes up a third of $GFS Revenue, could explain part of the surge in Volume seen on their OBV chart, instead of just a bet on the Photonics market; $GFS OBV has once again finished above it's Dec'22 ATH level, if it continues holding above past earnings, this stock will see ATHs again soon.
embedded_text:
If $QCOM sees a bottom in smartphone, it means there is also a bottom for $SOI and $GFS's core business.

If these see the weakest part of their business rebound while the bull thesis relies on photonics, there's no limit to what I'll tell my boss in a few months. https://t.co/KGNaFypf4D

For the first time, researchers at QuiX Quantum have demonstrated below threshold error mitigation in a photonic quantum computer. They used a technique called photon distillation, where multiple imperfect photons are combined through quantum interference to produce a cleaner, more identical photon. On a 20-mode photonic processor, this method reduced photon indistinguishability errors by 2.2× and achieved a 1.2× net reduction in total system error. That matters because quantum systems usually introduce more errors than they fix. This flips that balance. It could significantly reduce the number of photon sources needed, potentially by up to 4×, making photonic quantum computers far more practical 👀 In other words, this approach improves qubit quality at the source, bringing scalable, light based quantum computing much closer to reality."}}}, "rest_id": "2049521863710405103", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1823", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2049623614115246266", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "28", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
CPO, SILICON PHOTONICS, AND OPTICAL INTERCONNECT

CPO IS MOVING FROM R&D TO EARLY PRODUCTION, BUT THE NEAR-TERM DOLLARS REMAIN SMALL RELATIVE TO THE STRATEGIC SIGNAL (READ-THROUGH 10)

Call support: Management said FormFactor is “now beginning to ramp our TRITON production test system, co-developed with Advantest and Tokyo Electron,” and that the ramp is accelerating. Management attributed the acceleration to “growing volumes of CPO chips planned for later this year” and FormFactor’s leadership in Test Insertion 1, which ensures known-good die on the photonic integrated circuit wafer. Management also cautioned that CPO remains in “the very early innings” and that other insertions are “proving to be difficult for customers to implement in production.”

Affected companies: Advantest Corporation (6857: Japan) and Tokyo Electron Ltd. (8035: Japan) are positively affected with low near-term magnitude and higher long-duration optionality. The transmission mechanism is direct: TRITON was co-developed with these partners, and early CPO production test creates future attach opportunities for ATE, probers, and co-developed test platforms.

Broadcom Inc. (AVGO: US), Marvell Technology Inc. (MRVL: US), Coherent Corp. (COHR: US), Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE: US), and Fabrinet (FN: US) are positively affected with low near-term but potentially meaningful long-duration magnitude. The mechanism is validation of silicon photonics and CPO moving toward production use cases in AI networking. If CPO becomes necessary to address bandwidth density and power constraints, optical component suppliers, silicon photonics developers, and AI networking ASIC vendors benefit from a larger production ecosystem.

Teradyne Inc. (TER: US) is mixed to modestly positive. Management emphasized an open ecosystem and specifically said FormFactor works closely with Teradyne, but the named TRITON co-development partners were Advantest and Tokyo Electron. The mechanism is that Teradyne can still participate in future CPO insertions, but the earliest named production-test system appears more closely tied to Advantest and Tokyo Electron.

Near-term trading catalyst: positive for CPO sentiment but limited in revenue magnitude. The call supports the view that CPO is no longer purely a lab topic, but it does not indicate that CPO is yet a large 2026 revenue category for the broader supply chain.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: potentially high. The critical point is that Insertion 1 is focused on validating known-good photonic integrated circuits before expensive downstream packaging steps. If this becomes a standard production insertion, optical probing and electro-optical test can become recurring infrastructure layers in AI networking manufacturing.

DOWNSTREAM HARDWARE AND OEMS

AI SUPPLY-CHAIN TIGHTNESS IS POSITIVE FOR SEMICONDUCTOR VENDORS BUT NEGATIVE FOR HARDWARE OEM MARGIN FLEXIBILITY (READ-THROUGH 11)

Call support: Management described DRAM as supply constrained, HBM demand stepping up, networking demand strong, data-center CPU probe demand improving, CPO chip volumes planned later in 2026, and probe-card capacity constraints across FormFactor and competitors. The call also noted isolated cases where customers are willing to pay expedite fees in a capacity-constrained environment.

Affected companies: Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL: US), Hewlett Packard Enterprise Co. (HPE: US), Super Micro Computer Inc. (SMCI: US), Lenovo Group Ltd. (0992: Hong Kong), and HP Inc. (HPQ: US) are negatively affected with low-to-moderate magnitude on margin and delivery-risk grounds, while remaining positively exposed to AI server demand. The transmission mechanism is input-cost and availability pressure. If DRAM, HBM, probe cards, and advanced-packaging capacity remain tight, OEMs and server integrators face higher component costs, longer lead times, and reduced ability to optimize gross margin.

Apple Inc. (AAPL: US) is modestly negatively affected through memory availability and potential LPDDR/DRAM cost pressure. The mechanism is less directly AI-server related but still relevant if DRAM wafer starts are dynamically allocated across HBM, DDR5, DDR4, and other designs to maximize supplier margin.

Near-term trading catalyst: negative for gross-margin assumptions at hardware OEMs if memory price increases and AI component shortages persist.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: mixed. AI infrastructure demand supports server revenue growth, but the profit pool may continue migrating upstream toward memory suppliers, advanced-packaging providers, foundries, and test suppliers rather than downstream assemblers and OEMs.

CONSUMER SEMICONDUCTOR RECOVERY IS NOT THE DRIVER; AI INFRASTRUCTURE IS ABSORBING THE INCREMENTAL TEST AND CAPACITY DOLLARS (READ-THROUGH 12)

Call support: Management described Q2 Foundry & Logic growth as driven primarily by data-center CPU strength, with continued networking strength and “steady demand in PC and mobile.” The word “steady” is important because it contrasts with the stronger language used for HBM, networking, GPUs, custom ASICs, and CPO.

Affected companies: Qualcomm Inc. (QCOM: US), MediaTek Inc. (2454: Taiwan), Qorvo Inc. (QRVO: US), Skyworks Solutions Inc. (SWKS: US), and other mobile-exposed semiconductor suppliers are neutral to modestly negatively affected from a relative-growth perspective. The transmission mechanism is capital and capacity allocation. The call suggests incremental advanced test and probe demand is being pulled toward AI infrastructure rather than broad PC or mobile demand acceleration.

Apple Inc. (AAPL: US), HP Inc. (HPQ: US), and Lenovo Group Ltd. (0992: Hong Kong) are neutral to modestly negatively affected as end-market read-throughs. The mechanism is that upstream test demand is not indicating a strong consumer device rebound; PC and mobile are stable contributors, not the source of upside.

Near-term trading catalyst: negative for broad consumer semiconductor beta if investors are looking for evidence of a synchronized semiconductor recovery. The FormFactor call supports a narrower AI-led cycle.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: positive for AI infrastructure concentration and negative for the relative importance of consumer electronics in leading-edge semiconductor capital allocation. The call reinforces that the incremental complexity dollars are accruing to HBM, AI networking, CPU inference, GPU, custom ASIC, CPO, and advanced packaging rather than to traditional PC/mobile cycles.

text:
#SIVE $SIVE #5G #6G #Beamforming #FWA #SATCOM #CHIPSAct #Semiconductors #Wireless #Nokia

Take a moment to read this, friends — we’re going to talk about the unknown side of Sivers.

A lot of investors — and me first — only knew Sivers for its photonics business (the lasers for AI datacenters). 

It’s exciting and has massive potential. But after digging a bit deeper, we discover a real hidden gem: the Wireless division.

 It still accounts for ~70% of current revenue, delivered very strong growth (+85% on some quarters in 2025), and provides solid cash-flow stability.

 It’s the quiet but powerful pillar that makes the entire $SIVE investment thesis much less risky.

First of all, what is a beamformer? (simple explanation) : 

Imagine you want to send a super-fast internet signal (5G, future 6G, or satellite connection) to a phone, a house, or a satellite.

A regular radio signal spreads out in all directions, like a light bulb lighting up an entire room. It works, but it’s very inefficient. You waste a lot of energy, the signal weakens quickly over distance, and there are lots of interferences with other devices around.

A beamformer (literally “beam former”) is like an ultra-precise smart projector:
Instead of spreading everywhere, it concentrates all the signal power into a very narrow directional beam pointed exactly at your phone or your house.

Result? The connection is much faster, much more stable, reaches much farther… and uses way less energy.

Sivers Wireless develops exactly these mmWave beamformers (chips + antenna arrays). 
This technology is essential for high-speed 5G/6G, Fixed Wireless Access (FWA = wireless internet instead of fiber), SATCOM, and defense applications.

Major wins to date (April 2026):

• January 2025 → $5.4M contract (60 M SEK) signed with a global Tier-1 ( The crowd is murmering Nokia ? ) 

It’s for the next-generation beamforming transceivers. Even better: the collaboration is planned across multiple generations.

• New → Firm $3M purchase order with Tachyon Networks (28 GHz FWA). Production and shipments already starting in 2026 — real revenue incoming.

• 3 design wins in 5G/FWA already secured → potential of around 1 million units over the first three years of production (starting 2026).

• India → Historic partnership with Intel + WiSig Networks + office opened in Bangalore to target the massive FWA market.

• Defense → CHIPS Act funding (2nd round ongoing), contracts with Raytheon, BAE Systems, Ericsson. Daybreak™ 7-15 GHz ICs now available (March 2026) — very competitive in power and efficiency.

• SATCOM → Cloudchaser chipset + Maverick antenna arrays launched (Feb 2026) + strong commercial interest.

• 6G → Already involved in ISAC research with Nokia.

Why is this strategic for $SIVE?

Wireless brings stability and predictable revenues while the AI photonics division ramps into mass production (2026-2027). It’s the financial cushion that lets the company breathe… and it has strong potential to become a real cash cow from 2026-2028.

And even if photonics hits some delays or doesn’t deliver as expected, the Wireless division alone is strong enough to justify a major re-rating on its own.

In short: $SIVE is not just an AI laser story.

With the recent news of the Photonics spin-off to Silicon Valley, $SIVE (Sweden) is effectively becoming a pure-play leader in Wireless/SATCOM/Defense, while still holding the majority of the US AI laser powerhouse. It’s the ultimate value unlock.

It’s also a powerful play in mmWave 5G/6G, SATCOM, defense, and emerging markets — with technology that is already generating revenue.

I hope this clarified some things and/or brought new info for the Sivers community!

This is not financial advice. I’m just digging into this company and sharing what I found.

3238}, {"from_index": 3710, "richtext_types": ["Italic"], "to_index": 3800}]}, "text": "#SIVE $SIVE #5G #6G #Beamforming #FWA #SATCOM #CHIPSAct #Semiconductors #Wireless #Nokia Take a moment to read this, friends — we’re going to talk about the unknown side of Sivers. A lot of investors — and me first — only knew Sivers for its photonics business (the lasers for AI datacenters). It’s exciting and has massive potential. But after digging a bit deeper, we discover a real hidden gem: the Wireless division. It still accounts for ~70% of current revenue, delivered very strong growth (+85% on some quarters in 2025), and provides solid cash-flow stability. It’s the quiet but powerful pillar that makes the entire $SIVE investment thesis much less risky. First of all, what is a beamformer? (simple explanation) : Imagine you want to send a super-fast internet signal (5G, future 6G, or satellite connection) to a phone, a house, or a satellite. A regular radio signal spreads out in all directions, like a light bulb lighting up an entire room. It works, but it’s very inefficient. You waste a lot of energy, the signal weakens quickly over distance, and there are lots of interferences with other devices around. A beamformer (literally “beam former”) is like an ultra-precise smart projector: Instead of spreading everywhere, it concentrates all the signal power into a very narrow directional beam pointed exactly at your phone or your house. Result? The connection is much faster, much more stable, reaches much farther… and uses way less energy. Sivers Wireless develops exactly these mmWave beamformers (chips + antenna arrays). This technology is essential for high-speed 5G/6G, Fixed Wireless Access (FWA = wireless internet instead of fiber), SATCOM, and defense applications. Major wins to date (April 2026): • January 2025 → $5.4M contract (60 M SEK) signed with a global Tier-1 ( The crowd is murmering Nokia ? ) It’s for the next-generation beamforming transceivers. Even better: the collaboration is planned across multiple generations. • New → Firm $3M purchase order with Tachyon Networks (28 GHz FWA). Production and shipments already starting in 2026 — real revenue incoming. • 3 design wins in 5G/FWA already secured → potential of around 1 million units over the first three years of production (starting 2026). • India → Historic partnership with Intel + WiSig Networks + office opened in Bangalore to target the massive FWA market. • Defense → CHIPS Act funding (2nd round ongoing), contracts with Raytheon, BAE Systems, Ericsson. Daybreak™ 7-15 GHz ICs now available (March 2026) — very competitive in power and efficiency. • SATCOM → Cloudchaser chipset + Maverick antenna arrays launched (Feb 2026) + strong commercial interest. • 6G → Already involved in ISAC research with Nokia. Why is this strategic for $SIVE? Wireless brings stability and predictable revenues while the AI photonics division ramps into mass production (2026-2027). It’s the financial cushion that lets the company breathe… and it has strong potential to become a real cash cow from 2026-2028. And even if photonics hits some delays or doesn’t deliver as expected, the Wireless division alone is strong enough to justify a major re-rating on its own. In short: $SIVE is not just an AI laser story. With the recent news of the Photonics spin-off to Silicon Valley, $SIVE (Sweden) is effectively becoming a pure-play leader in Wireless/SATCOM/Defense, while still holding the majority of the US AI laser powerhouse. It’s the ultimate value unlock. It’s also a powerful play in mmWave 5G/6G, SATCOM, defense, and emerging markets — with technology that is already generating revenue. I hope this clarified some things and/or brought new info for the Sivers community! This is not financial advice. I’m just digging into this company and sharing what I found. #SIVE $SIVE #5G #6G #Beamforming #FWA #SATCOM #CHIPSAct #Semiconductors #Wireless #Nokia"}}}, "previous_counts": {"bookmark_count": 0, "favorite_count": 0, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 0}, "rest_id": "2049610156166570331", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "404", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
The most interesting pattern in AI photonics this year is not a fundamental story. It's a flow story. And it's already played out twice in the last six weeks. Something that popped up when I was doing research on $SIVE for an article that drops tomorrow.

$SOI Soitec. March 26: Morgan Stanley upgrades with photonics SOI as the bull case. April 22: they cross the 5% threshold and now hold 6.50% of the company, acquired through an off-market transaction. The same window saw multiple disclosed shorts from Two Sigma, Numeric, AHL Partners, and JPMorgan Asset Management.

$IQE. Up 323% in twelve months. Stockopedia tags it a Momentum Trap, trading 283% above its 200-day moving average. April 27: $MTSI MACOM leads an 81 million GBP strategic investment that ends IQE's strategic review.

The setup is the same in both names.

European compound semiconductor companies trade cheap on their home exchanges because local investors do not price the AI photonics optionality. US institutional desks notice. Analyst reports flag overvaluation. Retail and local holders sell into the perceived weakness. US institutions accumulate the float at the bottom of the shake-out. The fundamentals are not the trigger. The flow is.

Now look at $SIVE Sivers Semiconductors. Up over 900% in twelve months. Just announced a 1.6T transceiver collaboration with Jabil. Evaluating a Nasdaq New York dual listing. Stockopedia tags it a Momentum Trap. Analyst consensus 79% below market price.

Identical setup to where Soitec was in early March.

I want to be careful here. This is a hypothesis based on three data points. It tells you what could happen if the playbook repeats. It does not tell you when. It does not tell you whether the stock takes a 50% drawdown first. The companies are in different stages of maturity. Soitec has 890M EUR in revenue. Sivers has 30M USD, 72% of it NRE.

But the playbook is real. The receipts are public. It explains why analyst targets 80% below market, Momentum Trap classifications, and off-market transactions keep clustering in the same sub-sector at the same time.

I do not own any of these names. My framework is fundamentals, and I do not have edge in predicting where Morgan Stanley parks a 6.5% position three months from now. But the flow case is the most interesting non-fundamental thesis I have seen in AI infrastructure picks-and-shovels this year.

Worth watching closely.

text:
The AI Super Cycle rotates in phases

Semis like $ARM $AMD $INTC were Phase 1.

Money is already rotating:

Memory → $MU
Photonics / Optical → $NOK
Networking → $ANET
Compute → $IREN
Power → $VRT
Materials → $MP
Space → $ASTS
Defense / Drones → $KTOS $ONDS
Robotics → $TSLA

All you need to do is catch one part of one rotation of the cycle.

text:
No, Infleqtion and PsiQuantum are separate companies with different quantum tech approaches (neutral atoms vs. photonics).

Both are tenants at Chicago's Illinois Quantum & Microelectronics Park (IQMP). PsiQuantum is the anchor building a large photonic utility-scale system there. Infleqtion is developing its own neutral-atom quantum computer on the same campus as part of a $50M+ Illinois initiative. No direct affiliation between them.
@AIInvestorHQ Semiconductors and photonics are the future and there is nothing we can do about it

text:
$SIVE 
InP100 is a standardized platform of indium phosphide (InP) lasers and amplifier chips designed for direct integration with silicon photonics.

On‑chip light source: Enables placing the laser directly on the photonic die instead of in a separate package, reducing size, cost, and optical loss.

High single‑mode power: Delivers meaningful continuous‑wave output with narrow linewidth for better reach and signal quality.

Wafer‑scale flip‑chip readiness: Engineered for reproducible, high‑precision flip‑chip bonding to silicon or SiN waveguides.

Manufacturing friendly: Standardized epi processes and on‑wafer coatings support high yield and fast scale‑up to volume production.

Flexible form factors: Available as single emitters or compact arrays to suit different channel and power needs.

Core benefit: compact, lower‑cost, and scalable photonic systems with true on‑chip light sources, provided thermal and optical interfaces are co‑designed during integration.

text:
A lot of people still think $SIVE is just another early stage photonics story.

That view misses what is already happening under the surface. This is a company that has shipped tens of millions of devices and is producing at scale today. 

This is not a lab concept or a maybe in five years technology, it is real manufacturing with real deployment and real customers. 

What becomes even more interesting is where they sit in the stack. As AI infrastructure scales, the industry is running into hard physical limits, power, heat, bandwidth. One of the key architectural shifts being explored is the move toward external laser sources for optical interconnects. That is exactly where Sivers is positioned. If that approach becomes standard, they are not just another supplier, they become part of a critical bottleneck layer in next generation systems. 

And this is not easy technology to replicate. We are talking about sub micron manufacturing precision and extremely tight optical performance tolerances. That kind of capability is built over many years, and only a small number of companies globally can actually execute at that level. On top of that, one of the biggest risks in photonics, scaling production, has already been addressed. 

Through partnerships with foundries like WIN Semiconductor, there is already a path toward high volume manufacturing. That removes a major uncertainty that holds many companies in this space back. At the same time, they are quietly embedded in the global photonics ecosystem. Collaborations with research leaders like IMEC place them inside the same innovation network that feeds the broader semiconductor industry. 

That kind of positioning often means early access to what comes next and a role in shaping it. 

Put all of this together and the picture looks very different from the usual speculative small cap narrative. 

It starts to look more like a scaled and technically entrenched player sitting right at the intersection of where AI infrastructure is heading next.

text:
$SIVE 
The market underestimates when deep tech IP actually starts to monetize. That’s exactly what we’re seeing with Sivers Semiconductors: years of investment in laser and photonics R&D that are only now reaching a scaling phase with CPO (co-packaged optics) and AI data center demand. Retail tends to give up during the long wait, while institutions step in right when visibility improves.

The “playbook” you’re describing isn’t imaginary. With Soitec and IQE, we saw similar setups: weak sentiment, skeptical analyst coverage, followed by accumulation from large players like Morgan Stanley or Point72 before a major rerating.

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2439}, {"from_index": 2465, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2499}]}, "text": "What makes a company truly compelling is not just what it is today, but how many powerful trends it could end up riding at once. That is a big part of why $SIVE / $SIVEF stands out to me. Most investors build exposure one theme at a time. They buy one company for AI, another for defense, another for satcom, another for photonics, and maybe yet another for next-generation telecom. It is a bit like assembling the future one puzzle piece at a time, hoping the picture eventually comes together. Sivers feels different. It feels more like finding one piece that already connects several of the most important parts of that picture. At first glance, it is easy to label it as just another semiconductor company. But the more I look at it, the more that feels like an oversimplification. Sivers sits in the overlap between mmWave, photonics, datacenter connectivity, satcom and defense. Those are not niche or isolated markets. They are major long-term themes, and the company has exposure to several of them at once. That is what makes the setup so compelling. Sivers does not need every single door to open at the same time for the story to work. It may be enough for one or two of these markets to accelerate, one or two partnerships to deepen, or one part of the business to gain meaningful commercial traction before investors begin to see the company in a very different light. That is why I think the “one stock, several themes” angle matters here. For a new investor, Sivers may be easier to understand if you stop thinking about it as a single narrow bet and start thinking about it as a small platform with multiple shots on goal. Not one straight road, but a crossroads. Not one engine, but several potential drivers of value that could begin contributing at different times. Of course, that does not remove the risk. Stories with several moving parts are never simple. But they can also create exactly the kind of asymmetry that makes small-cap investing so interesting, especially when the market has not fully decided how to categorize the company yet. And that is often where the upside starts to emerge. So the appeal of Sivers, to me, is not just that it has exposure to attractive end markets. It is that you do not need to buy five different companies to get that exposure. You are looking at one company that could potentially benefit from several major technology shifts at once. That kind of setup is rare. And that is why I think Sivers deserves a much closer look."}}}, "rest_id": "2045914937076183188", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "245064", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2050295351144333712", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "351", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Cross-entropy at light speed? Nonlinear log is the killer—pure photonics struggles there without detection. 

Idea: Capture softmax probs as intensities on a detector array, then pipe into nonlinear optical elements (saturable absorbers or SOA chips) to approximate the log via intensity-dependent transmission. Modulate one-hot labels onto a parallel laser beam, interfere/combine for the -y log(p) products, and sum optically via passive couplers. 

Feasible in theory with wave-mixing crystals, but noisy and low-precision vs hybrid (optics forward, electronics loss). Early optical NN papers hint at approximations like this. What part are you optimizing for?

@grok @GoodmanAric @grok Do you have any ideas for how to do gradient descent this way? How do we… “record”… into a holographic medium the cross entropy component? How do we do cross entropy with photonics, what might the recording process look like?

text:
The next $SIVE is probably in South Korea

I missed the $SIVE and the broader photonics run. By the time I finished my DD after seeing @aleabitoreddit's recommendation, it was already too late as it ran too high too fast.

Since then I have been scouring the market for similar opportunities that hasn't run as much as it did.

Specifically, I went hunting in Korea. Cheaper market, less analyst noise, and small-caps that the West hasn't gotten around to mispricing yet.

That's where I found OE Solutions. KOSDAQ ticker $138080.KQ. A Korean optical transceiver company that, the more I dug, kept giving me the strange feeling that I was reading an earlier version of the Sivers thesis--except this one already runs a fab at volume, ships product to named telecom customers, and trades at a fraction of the multiple.

A few things I now believe to be true after re-checking each line against primary sources where I could.

OE Solutions did about KRW 57.4bn in 2025 revenue (~$40m). Sivers reported SEK 304.1m in full-year 2025 revenue (~$30m). So the smaller-cap company does more revenue.

Market caps. As of early May 2026, Sivers sits at roughly SEK 15-16.5bn (~$1.5-1.7bn). OE Solutions sits at roughly KRW 458bn (~$340m at current FX). So OE has higher revenue at roughly one-fifth of Sivers' market cap. The price-to-sales gap is the headline.

Sell-side expects OE to swing to operating profit in 2026. Hana Securities is the most explicit on this--target raised from KRW 30,000 to KRW 50,000, "buy" rating maintained. Sivers, on consensus, is still loss-making in 2026. Treat OE's profit turn as sell-side opinion, not company guidance, but the directional gap is real.

Both companies make Indium Phosphide lasers, the enabling component for high-speed optical interconnects in AI data centers. The right framing isn't "chokepoint material"--it's that scaled InP suppliers are few, wafer yields are constrained, and demand is running ahead of capacity.

Where the two companies sit in the stack is different.

Sivers operates a 100mm InP wafer fab in Glasgow (acquired via CST Global in 2017). According to Sivers' own materials, the facility ships around 1 million lasers per month, with over 45 million lasers cumulatively in the field. They are not a small-scale supplier. To scale further for AI data center volumes, Sivers announced an OEM partnership with WIN Semiconductors in Taoyuan in March 2025--an outsourced foundry arrangement to expand DFB laser output. So the capacity story is "established volume supplier scaling further."

OE Solutions, per its own 2024 IR deck, runs vertical integration from epitaxy through chip fabrication to transceiver assembly out of its Gwangju facility. Korean industry reporting describes OE as mass-producing chips today and using its own laser diodes inside its own transceivers--with secondary reporting suggesting external chip sales to Chinese customers since 2024. I could not find a primary disclosure that puts an exact wafer-per-month number on the OE fab. So treat the comparison as: Sivers ships components into other people's modules, OE makes the modules itself. Different position in the stack, both with their own fabs.

Customers. Sivers has named, primary-source partnerships: Jabil for a 1.6T LRO transceiver collaboration announced April 15, 2026; POET Technologies for external light source modules, with prototypes targeted for H1 2026 and production-readiness for end-2026 (per Sivers' own Q4 2025 report, qualification with POET is ongoing); Ayar Labs going back to ECOC 2022; https://t.co/VKc0iGZ1CB in SATCOM. Most are real but development-stage.

OE Solutions is named in Korean industry reporting as supplying Samsung, Nokia, Ericsson, Cisco and Ciena, with NTT-bound FTTH volume coming through a Sumitomo handover. There are reports from Korea saying OE began mass-supplying 10G EPON OLT transceivers for NTT. I could not find a clean OE press release proving every one of those names is currently shipping, so the right characterization is "named in industry reporting as a customer of," not "verified invoice-paying." That said, the 2025 revenue jump of nearly 80% YoY needs to come from somewhere--it's not coming from thin air.

Both companies are positioned for the 1.6T transition. Sivers via the Jabil collaboration. OE via a 23 dBm cooled ELSFP module for CPO external light sources, with sampling targeted for Q3 2026 (this one is on OE's own blog as a primary source). Korean reporting also mentions a 1.6T OSFP unveiled at OFC 2026, but I couldn't surface OE's own press release for that exact product, so I'd hedge that line.

Sivers' stock is up roughly 1200%+ over the past twelve months. The thesis the market paid for is "InP laser scarcity, scaling capacity, AI data center exposure." That thesis worked.

The OE thesis rhymes. Same InP laser scarcity. Vertical integration that Sivers is now pursuing through outsourced partners. Earnings inflection driven by something more concrete than a hyperscaler win--the Sumitomo to NTT FTTH volume handover, which Korean industry reporting describes as roughly one-third reflected in current numbers, with the rest expected to flow through over the next several quarters. Treat that one-third figure as secondary reporting, not a number from a filing.

So why does the gap exist?

Sivers is European-listed and pursuing a Nasdaq dual listing--Western analyst coverage, English-language Substack writeups, the whole machinery. OE Solutions trades on KOSDAQ. The Korean small-cap discount is real and persistent. OE's investor disclosures page also shows repeated CB conversion entries through March-April 2026, which is exactly the dilution overhang you'd expect to weigh on the multiple.

These are real risks. I'm not waving them away.

But the valuation gap is too wide for the operational gap.

If OE Solutions gets even half of Sivers' price-to-sales multiple, applied to a revenue base that grows the way sell-side expects, you're looking at multiples of the current market cap. Pure rerating math. No new news required. And if the AI data center qualifications already in flight start landing--ITLA modules with Photon Bridge, the ELSFP sample shipments, and whatever materializes at the higher data rates--the rerating compounds.

The asymmetry is what matters. The Sumitomo handover and the laser diode internalization carry the stock to a meaningful re-rate without anything heroic happening. The data center catalyst is a free option on top of that. If it hits, it hits the way Sivers did. If it doesn't, the earnings inflection still pays you.

I missed Sivers. At Korean market open, I opened a position in OE Solutions.

Not financial advice. Do your own due diligence.

I missed the $SIVE and the broader photonics run. By the time I finished my DD after seeing @aleabitoreddit's recommendation, it was already too late as it ran too high too fast.

Since then I have been scouring the market for similar opportunities that hasn't run as much as it did.

Specifically, I went hunting in Korea. Cheaper market, less analyst noise, and small-caps that the West hasn't gotten around to mispricing yet.

That's where I found OE Solutions. KOSDAQ ticker $138080.KQ. A Korean optical transceiver company that, the more I dug, kept giving me the strange feeling that I was reading an earlier version of the Sivers thesis--except this one already runs a fab at volume, ships product to named telecom customers, and trades at a fraction of the multiple.

A few things I now believe to be true after re-checking each line against primary sources where I could.

OE Solutions did about KRW 57.4bn in 2025 revenue (~$40m). Sivers reported SEK 304.1m in full-year 2025 revenue (~$30m). So the smaller-cap company does more revenue.

Market caps. As of early May 2026, Sivers sits at roughly SEK 15-16.5bn (~$1.5-1.7bn). OE Solutions sits at roughly KRW 458bn (~$340m at current FX). So OE has higher revenue at roughly one-fifth of Sivers' market cap. The price-to-sales gap is the headline.

Sell-side expects OE to swing to operating profit in 2026. Hana Securities is the most explicit on this--target raised from KRW 30,000 to KRW 50,000, "buy" rating maintained. Sivers, on consensus, is still loss-making in 2026. Treat OE's profit turn as sell-side opinion, not company guidance, but the directional gap is real.

Both companies make Indium Phosphide lasers, the enabling component for high-speed optical interconnects in AI data centers. The right framing isn't "chokepoint material"--it's that scaled InP suppliers are few, wafer yields are constrained, and demand is running ahead of capacity.

Where the two companies sit in the stack is different.

Sivers operates a 100mm InP wafer fab in Glasgow (acquired via CST Global in 2017). According to Sivers' own materials, the facility ships around 1 million lasers per month, with over 45 million lasers cumulatively in the field. They are not a small-scale supplier. To scale further for AI data center volumes, Sivers announced an OEM partnership with WIN Semiconductors in Taoyuan in March 2025--an outsourced foundry arrangement to expand DFB laser output. So the capacity story is "established volume supplier scaling further."

OE Solutions, per its own 2024 IR deck, runs vertical integration from epitaxy through chip fabrication to transceiver assembly out of its Gwangju facility. Korean industry reporting describes OE as mass-producing chips today and using its own laser diodes inside its own transceivers--with secondary reporting suggesting external chip sales to Chinese customers since 2024. I could not find a primary disclosure that puts an exact wafer-per-month number on the OE fab. So treat the comparison as: Sivers ships components into other people's modules, OE makes the modules itself. Different position in the stack, both with their own fabs.

Customers. Sivers has named, primary-source partnerships: Jabil for a 1.6T LRO transceiver collaboration announced April 15, 2026; POET Technologies for external light source modules, with prototypes targeted for H1 2026 and production-readiness for end-2026 (per Sivers' own Q4 2025 report, qualification with POET is ongoing); Ayar Labs going back to ECOC 2022; https://t.co/VKc0iGZ1CB in SATCOM. Most are real but development-stage.

OE Solutions is named in Korean industry reporting as supplying Samsung, Nokia, Ericsson, Cisco and Ciena, with NTT-bound FTTH volume coming through a Sumitomo handover. There are reports from Korea saying OE began mass-supplying 10G EPON OLT transceivers for NTT. I could not find a clean OE press release proving every one of those names is currently shipping, so the right characterization is "named in industry reporting as a customer of," not "verified invoice-paying." That said, the 2025 revenue jump of nearly 80% YoY needs to come from somewhere--it's not coming from thin air.

Both companies are positioned for the 1.6T transition. Sivers via the Jabil collaboration. OE via a 23 dBm cooled ELSFP module for CPO external light sources, with sampling targeted for Q3 2026 (this one is on OE's own blog as a primary source). Korean reporting also mentions a 1.6T OSFP unveiled at OFC 2026, but I couldn't surface OE's own press release for that exact product, so I'd hedge that line.

Sivers' stock is up roughly 1200%+ over the past twelve months. The thesis the market paid for is "InP laser scarcity, scaling capacity, AI data center exposure." That thesis worked.

The OE thesis rhymes. Same InP laser scarcity. Vertical integration that Sivers is now pursuing through outsourced partners. Earnings inflection driven by something more concrete than a hyperscaler win--the Sumitomo to NTT FTTH volume handover, which Korean industry reporting describes as roughly one-third reflected in current numbers, with the rest expected to flow through over the next several quarters. Treat that one-third figure as secondary reporting, not a number from a filing.

So why does the gap exist?

Sivers is European-listed and pursuing a Nasdaq dual listing--Western analyst coverage, English-language Substack writeups, the whole machinery. OE Solutions trades on KOSDAQ. The Korean small-cap discount is real and persistent. OE's investor disclosures page also shows repeated CB conversion entries through March-April 2026, which is exactly the dilution overhang you'd expect to weigh on the multiple.

These are real risks. I'm not waving them away.

But the valuation gap is too wide for the operational gap.

If OE Solutions gets even half of Sivers' price-to-sales multiple, applied to a revenue base that grows the way sell-side expects, you're looking at multiples of the current market cap. Pure rerating math. No new news required. And if the AI data center qualifications already in flight start landing--ITLA modules with Photon Bridge, the ELSFP sample shipments, and whatever materializes at the higher data rates--the rerating compounds.

The asymmetry is what matters. The Sumitomo handover and the laser diode internalization carry the stock to a meaningful re-rate without anything heroic happening. The data center catalyst is a free option on top of that. If it hits, it hits the way Sivers did. If it doesn't, the earnings inflection still pays you.

I missed Sivers. At Korean market open, I opened a position in OE Solutions.

Not financial advice. Do your own due diligence."}}}, "rest_id": "2051422076989505927", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "10080", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

deep dive:

The tech is real. The management is radioactive. Here's why this is the most important bear case in photonics right now.

POET Technologies makes optical interposers — silicon wafers that let you snap photonic components into place without manual alignment. That's a 75% cost reduction on the most expensive part of building optical transceivers. If it works at scale, it's a real bottleneck-solver for AI data centers.

The problem isn't the engineering. It's everything else.

Revenue: $1.07 million trailing twelve months. That's not a typo. Q4 revenue was $341,202 — all prototype fees, zero product revenue. The company lost $42.7 million that same quarter. EBITDA is negative $39 million.

Yet the market cap is over $1 billion. That's 700x+ EV/revenue. For context, Marvell trades at 15-20x, Lumentum at 9-15x, Coherent at 9x. POET is priced like a scaled platform company that doesn't exist yet.

The cash position is real — $430 million after massive dilution (they raised $525 million in the last two quarters). So no bankruptcy risk. But the dilution story is brutal: they funded a pre-revenue R&D lab by printing shares, which created the PFIC tax problem.

Here's where it gets ugly. On April 14, Wolfpack Research published a short report flagging POET as a likely Passive Foreign Investment Company under US tax law — punitive tax treatment for American shareholders. The CFO called it a "nothing burger" on a YouTube livestream.

Two days later, he publicly disclosed Marvell's confidential purchase orders on that same YouTube channel. Marvell formally canceled all contracts on April 23, citing breach of NDA. That was POET's most validated commercial pipeline — gone in 48 hours because the CFO couldn't stop talking to retail stock promoters.

The stock dropped 47% on the cancellation news. A federal class action lawsuit was filed April 29. Insider selling hit $400K in the 90 days before the collapse.

Risks: PFIC tax status unresolved (if confirmed, institutional capital flees). Management credibility destroyed — LITEON and Luxshare are now watching a partner who leaked Marvell's supply chain data. Malaysian manufacturing ramp at Globetronics is unproven — passive alignment yields at volume are the key assumption, and they haven't demonstrated it yet. Class action litigation is ongoing.

The technology has genuine acquisition value — the patent portfolio on wafer-level optical testing is defensible, and the ferroelectric memory research (published in Nature Communications, co-authored by the CEO) addresses the AI memory wall. But the equity vehicle is toxic at any price above distressed multiples.

Verdict: SELL — brilliant IP trapped inside a governance disaster. Watch the May 13 earnings call: if the board doesn't fire the CFO, it signals terminal corporate rot.

@WolfpackRes called this one out.

Idk, I feel like all the pieces are there for this to happen technologically, you'd think somebody out there has to be throwing some decent money at it

text:
My exposure to photonics 

*You've basically called all of them

Think these are positioned well to capture capex ramp over the next 3-5 years 

SIVE 
- Sivers Semiconductors (lasers for CPO optical engines)

OE Solutions  
- InP lasers + ELSFP modules for CPO

LPK (LPKF)    
- Laser systems for glass interposers & advanced photonics packaging

ALMU          
- Aeluma (compound semiconductor photonics / quantum dot lasers)

Episil Precision (https://t.co/TuTwLjmIHE) 
- Silicon photonics epi wafers

IQE 
- Compound semi epi wafers (InP/GaAs/GaN) for photonics

SOI (Soitec)  
- Engineered substrates (SOI) critical for silicon photonics

ShunShin (https://t.co/f1ZwK4ljHn) 
- Optical packaging & assembly for CPO

text:
$inv
Reposting again 

Everyone still has eyes on the memory and photonics  trade $SNDK $MU $DRAM $LITE $COHR $SIVE

Whilst there might be some more room in these still, they are becoming somewhat crowded. 

Exactly why Goldman is saying liquid cooling is the next trade like memory. 

Liquid cooling has lagged the overall data centre trade.

All the private cooling companies are getting hoovered up at the moment in big private deals. $ETN and Boyd. $ECL and Cool IT. $VRT and Strategic Thermal Labs. 
These big companies are trying to build out their end to end solutions for next gen cooling. 

Then you’ve got the Series B round of $jci in @AccelsiusATX $inv

It’s actually hard to get exposure to a public pure play cooling company right now. 
I think $inv is the closest way with there 43.2% holding in @AccelsiusATX 

From my research it’s abundantly clear the direction liquid cooling is going.

$inv
From the GS global equites call this AM

They expect liquid cooling to be the next bottle neck. Similar to memory last year $mu $sndk $5930.sk SK Hynix

A great example of this was: the memory theme that emerged late last year. The current bottlenecks now exist in fiber https://t.co/B6Ws7wS5lD

What stock is gonna be next?

The same thing applies to AI.

There are shortages in GPUs, CPUs, data centers, power generation, photonics, chemicals, and much more.

These imbalances won't go away anytime soon.

text:
The opportunity cost of this now is massive.

If you are relatively well off, go ahead- do your own yard work. 

Could have been trading photonics stocks and made 300%
embedded_text:
The entire "hard work" or "rugged" ideology was perhaps the most charming trait of the old WASP class. 

Presidents like Reagan and Bush were always being photographed cutting brush or something. 

You don't see that with Obama, Trump, etc. https://t.co/uUU3XtC0dh

text:
The Full Optical AI Industry Chain:  

1. IC Design & Manufacturing  
(Where the brains are built)

ASIC / xPU / Photonic ICs  
→ $NVDA $MRVL $AVGO $LITE $COHR $INTC $AMD $CSCO  

Wafer Foundries  
→ $TSM $TSEM $GFS $UMC  

2. Materials (The Overlooked Layer)  
(The foundation of photonics)  

Indium Phosphide  
→ $AXTI $IQE  

Gallium Arsenide  
→ $AXTI $MTSI  

This layer is underfollowed and could see strong upside as optical demand expands.

3. Optical Components  
(The data highways)  

VCSEL / EML Lasers  
→ $LITE $COHR $AVGO $AAOI  

Optical Module Assembly  
→ $FN $COHR $LITE $MRVL $CSCO  

Fiber / WDM / Optical Connectivity  
→ $CIEN $GLW $LITE  

MPO Connectors  
→ $GLW $APH  

Optical Fiber  
→ $GLW  

4. Packaging & Testing  
(The hidden backbone)  

Packaging  
→ $ASX $AMKR $FN $JBL  

Packaging Equipment  
→ $KLIC 

Testing  
→ $TER $KEYS $FORM $AEHR

As AI clusters scale into hundreds of thousands of GPUs, the challenge becomes moving data faster, farther, and with less power. Copper is hitting its limits. Optical is the next layer of scale. Why optical matters: • Higher bandwidth • Lower latency • Lower power consumption • Less heat • Better long-distance transmission That’s why hyperscalers are aggressively investing in optical-heavy infrastructure. Core optical AI plays on my radar: $COHR — broad optical exposure $LITE — datacenter optical leader $AAOI — optical modules growth $FN — manufacturing backbone $MRVL — AI networking silicon $CIEN — optical transport infrastructure AI compute gets the attention. Optical networking moves the data. Without optical interconnects, AI doesn’t scale. Follow the data pipes, not just the chips. text:
✅ قائمة المراقبة الدورية للـ AI (النسخة الكاملة)

يهذي الدورة الكبرى للذكاء الاصطناعي اللي لسى معظم الناس ما انتبهوا لها:

السيميكوندكترز (Semis): $NVDA $AMD $ARM $AVGO $MRVL $INTC $TSM $QCOM

الذاكرة (Memory): $MU $WDC $STX $SNDK

الفوتونيكس والألياف البصرية (Photonics / Optical): $LITE $COHR $AAOI $GLW $JBL $AXTI $LWLG $NOK

الشبكات (Networking): $ANET $CSCO $CIEN $JNPR

الحوسبة ومراكز البيانات (Compute / Data Centers): $IREN $CIFR $NBIS

الطاقة والشبكة الكهربائية (Power / Grid): $VRT $ETN $PWR $GEV $CEG $SMR $OKLO

المواد والمعادن (Materials / Minerals): $MP $LAC $UUUU $USAR

الفضاء والأقمار الصناعية (Space / Satellites): $ASTS $RKLB $LUNR $PL $BKSY

الدفاع والدرونز (Defense / Drones): $AVAV $KTOS $ONDS $OSS
الروبوتات والـ AI (Robotics / AI): $TSLA $PATH $SYM

هذا بالضبط مسار تدفق الفلوس ودورانها في هالسوبر سايكل.

text:
$LWLG is poised for explosive growth with an unmatched competitive edge that positions the company as a clear leader in next-generation photonics for AI-driven data centers and high-speed communications. 

With over 70 issued U.S. and international patents plus dozens more pending, they enjoy complete bulletproof freedom-to-operate across their entire suite of electro-optic polymer materials and devices, eliminating any risk of IP challenges and safeguarding their path to market dominance. 

Their state-of-the-art in-house manufacturing operation right in Denver, Colorado, produces rare-earth-free, fully supply-chain-resilient polymers that are already scaled and ready for high-volume production, giving them total control over quality and eliminating the geopolitical vulnerabilities that plague competitors reliant on overseas critical materials. 

Now in 2026, the company has successfully completed its full transition from pure R&D into commercialization mode, laser-focused on delivering the exceptional reliability and thermal stability required for seamless integration into leading foundry processes like Silicon Photonics. 

At the core of this breakthrough are their proprietary Perkinamine polymers, which deliver superior electro-optic performance with blazing bandwidths beyond 200 Gbps per lane, dramatically lower power consumption, and industry-first reliability metrics that have passed rigorous Telcordia GR-468 testing. 

This powerful combination of ironclad intellectual property, domestic manufacturing independence, and polymers engineered for real-world foundry deployment creates a formidable moat that will drive significant market penetration and shareholder value as demand for ultra-efficient optical interconnects surges in the AI era.

text:
$SIVE was a massive transfer from Swedish locals to US investors.

Locals waited many, many years for all the laser R&D to pay off for CPO and silicon photonics. 

And now with hyperscaler volume ramp H2 2026 H1 2027, they don’t get any upside because they transferred their shares over to the US/West after trusting local media.

My prediction is if and when Sivers hits $4-8B on NASDAQ, we’ll get the same media treatment in the US.

Then the shares get transferred from US retail to US institutions.

You’ve already seen the same playbook with $IQE and $SOI at the bottom… with institutions posting “analyst reports” saying Soitec was overvalued. 

The next month they go up 3x as Morgan Stanley happens to pick up 6.5% of the $SOI float… or Point72 with $IQE.

Institutions are not your friends. I’m looking out for retail’s best interest though.

text:
He's talking about **Lightelligence** (official name: Shanghai Xizhi Technology / 曦智科技, HK: 1879). 

That's the Chinese optical computing/photonics company that just IPO'd in Hong Kong, popped ~380-400% on debut, and is developing CPO solutions for Biren. The "Xi" in the name is from Xizhi (曦智). The image in the post is their PACE 2 optoelectronic accelerator card.

text:
Jensen's 5-Layer AI framework is the cleanest investment thesis you'll see in 2026. Energy. Compute. Photonics. Memory. Chips. Every layer is in shortage. Every layer is repricing higher.

Layer 1: Energy. AI training campuses pulling 1+ gigawatts each. Data center power demand growing 40 percent annually. Bloom Energy, Oklo, Vertiv. Bull thesis is small modular reactors and fuel cells get the AI buildout contracts. Bear thesis is grid backlogs delay the buildout 3 years.

Layer 2: Compute. Hyperscaler GPU clusters can't keep up. CoreWeave, Nebius, IREN, Cipher. The neoclouds capture overflow demand at premium pricing. Bull case is 5x revenue. Bear case is hyperscalers self-supply and squeeze them out.

Layer 3: Photonics. Networking inside data centers is the new bottleneck. Applied Optoelectronics, Lumentum, Coherent, Astera Labs. 800G and 1.6T optical transceivers sold out through 2027. Bull case is networking captures real margin. Bear case is hyperscalers vertically integrate optics.

Layer 4: Memory. The bottleneck inside the bottleneck. Micron, SanDisk, Seagate, plus SK Hynix and Samsung internationally. DA Davidson just put a $1,000 PT on Micron. Bull case is structurally extended cycle. Bear case is China NAND oversupply.

Layer 5: Chips. NVDA, AMD, Broadcom, plus custom silicon at Google, Amazon, Meta. Bull case is NVDA's CUDA moat holds 5 more years. Bear case is custom silicon takes 30 percent share by 2028 and the stack price-compresses.

What nobody's saying: this isn't a "buy everything AI" framework.

It's a structural map of where shortages compound. Every layer has supply tight through 2027. Pick the layer with the deepest moat and the cheapest valuation.

My answer: memory at Layer 4. Energy at Layer 1.

The map is real.

Pick your shovels.

Layer 4: Memory. The bottleneck inside the bottleneck. Micron, SanDisk, Seagate, plus SK Hynix and Samsung internationally. DA Davidson just put a $1,000 PT on Micron. Bull case is structurally extended cycle. Bear case is China NAND oversupply.

Layer 5: Chips. NVDA, AMD, Broadcom, plus custom silicon at Google, Amazon, Meta. Bull case is NVDA's CUDA moat holds 5 more years. Bear case is custom silicon takes 30 percent share by 2028 and the stack price-compresses.

What nobody's saying: this isn't a "buy everything AI" framework.

It's a structural map of where shortages compound. Every layer has supply tight through 2027. Pick the layer with the deepest moat and the cheapest valuation.

My answer: memory at Layer 4. Energy at Layer 1.

The map is real.

Pick your shovels.

today: 
"wins a $21M Texas grant to expand semiconductor and photonics manufacturing in Sugar Land.  The project adds a 210K sq. ft. facility for AI data center transceiver production and is expected to create 500+ jobs." https://t.co/wKWAy76O36

Foxconn Is Ready to Embrace CPO, as Silicon Photonic Products Manage to Reach 3.2T Transmission Capacity 

On the 29th, Guo Haozhong, director of the Foxconn Semiconductor Research Institute, delivered a speech on silicon photonics trends at the 2026 MIC FORUM Spring "Intelligent New Order" seminar. He said that Foxconn's (2317) silicon photonics products can already achieve a transmission capacity of 3.2T, but with the continued shortage of InP capacity, silicon photonics will still face 10 challenges to overcome in the future.

This year, the slogan for AI servers has changed from "fiber to copper" to "fiber and copper in parallel". This is mainly because Broadcom chips have optimized the 200G transmission speed. However, copper wires will become increasingly difficult to use, especially high-speed copper wires which are heavy and require external sheathing. The advantage of optical communication is that a single wire can carry multiple wavelengths simultaneously without interfering with each other, up to a maximum of eight wavelengths.

Guo Haozhong stated that Foxconn's modulator can already achieve a single-channel transmission speed of 200G, using 2 or 4 different wavelength lasers and employing WDM (wavelength division multiplexing) technology to integrate on a PIC (photonic integrated circuit) chip; for a single-channel 200G, if two groups of four wavelengths are used, a transmission capacity of 3.2T can be achieved, and it even has the ability to upgrade the technology to 6.4T in the future.
Since future data center transmission speeds will reach over 400G, he stated that the current single-channel transmission rate of silicon photonics is limited to nearly 200G and has high power requirements. InP has advantages in high-speed transmission, not only integrating lasers but also helping to support transmission of systems above 400G. However, InP material is not as stable as silicon, resulting in low yield and high cost. Currently, due to insufficient production capacity, many companies are also investing in the research and development of other materials, such as TFLN or SOH, which have become materials of interest.

InP has advantages in high-speed transmission, not only integrating lasers but also helping to support transmission of systems above 400G. {"from_index": 2228, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2257}, {"from_index": 2423, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2467}]}, "text": "I think one of the most underestimated parts of the Sivers / $SIVE story is the SATCOM opportunity with https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp. This is not just another customer relationship. Sivers has been working with https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp for years through several development programs worth many millions of dollars, and the relationship appears to go much deeper than a normal supplier agreement. Sivers is part of the technology roadmap for advanced satellite terminals that are built for a world where connectivity will move across different orbits, networks and operators. That is where the potential becomes interesting. According to $SIVE own material, a SATCOM terminal can contain up to around 1,800 Sivers chips, with potential Sivers value of up to around $10,000 per terminal. That changes the way you should think about scale. This is not a consumer hardware story where millions of units are needed before revenue matters. In this case, even relatively small deployment numbers can become meaningful very quickly. If https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp sells 1,000 terminals, that is up to around $10 million for Sivers. At 5,000 terminals, up to around $50 million. At 10,000 terminals, up to around $100 million. And that is from one customer platform. This is why I think the market may be underestimating SATCOM. The focus is often on when Wireless can become meaningful again, but the answer may not only come from huge 5G volumes. It could also come from a smaller number of high-value satellite terminals where Sivers has very high value per terminal. The timing also matters. $SIVE has indicated that volume ramp is expected in H2 2026. If that happens, SATCOM moves from development revenue into something much more important: recurring product revenue tied to actual terminal deployments. And the backdrop is strong. https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp is already working with demanding customers and partners such as the US Army, NATO-related defence programs, Viasat, SES and Intelsat, while defence demand for resilient satellite communication is increasing and commercial networks are moving toward more advanced multi-orbit connectivity. Terminals need to become smarter, more capable and more complex. That is exactly where Sivers beamforming technology becomes valuable. For me, this is one of the most interesting parts of the $SIVE story into 2026. Not because it requires massive volumes. But because in SATCOM, small volumes can already become big numbers. #SATCOM #Beamforming #SatelliteCommunications #SpaceTech #DefenceTech #MultiOrbit #SIVEF"}}}, "rest_id": "2049351404595163589", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "18215", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2049540957603831884", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "638", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Laser Photonics $LASE, yaklaşık 5.7 milyon adet varantın $0.70 fiyatla kullanılmasıyla ~4 milyon dolar nakit elde etti. 

Bunun karşılığında yatırımcılara, $0.975 kullanım fiyatlı yeni varantlar (Series A-5 ve A-6) verildi.

 Bu yeni varantlar hissedar onayı sonrası devreye girecek ve 2–5 yıl vadeli olacak.

text:
Arrogance is costly.

Some of the highly regarded Korean photonics crew are about to get punished… because they’re still looking at charts on a 15-minute / yearly basis.

https://t.co/IIoZNOe7gU

“Price is king” is a lesson they still need to learn.
Warned this guy earlier (tweet below).
But we all learn at our own pace.

The real lesson: solid analyst teams and influencers with great picks, but limited experience pumping overbought stocks to their followers right before a 40–50% correction.

$IQE is headed to the 23–28 range.
We’ll be buyers then.

text:
We will be taking a short position in Semis here.
Short trade expected to last a couple of weeks. After that, we’ll look to pick up bottoms in select photonics names following a solid correction/profit-taking phase.

Trade setup:
- Using SMH, SPXL, and SOXS
- Earnings pop into mega tech earnings today
- Ideal entry on SOXS in the $13.60–$14.25 range
- Targeting 25–30% return over the next 2 weeks

Some photonic stocks will likely sell off hard (some already started) - which should create a good buying opportunity for those who missed the big rally.

Stay tuned.

text:
The optics bottleneck thesis is no longer fringe. It’s becoming one of the clearest AI infrastructure pressure points. LightCounting has the AI-cluster optical interconnect market moving from ~$5B in 2024 to $10B+ by 2026. Silicon photonics chip sales projected from ~$0.8B in 2023 to $3B+ by 2029.

Cignal AI had 2025 datacom optical components growing 60%+ to over $16B, with 800G transceiver shipments roughly doubling YoY and 1.6T entering production for Nvidia in 2026.

Dell’Oro sees 2026 as the first real volume year for 1.6T switches, with the ramp potentially passing 5M ports within 1–2 years, faster than 800G.

The demand backdrop is pretty obvious: Meta + Corning signed a multi-year agreement worth up to $6B to accelerate hyperscale data center buildout.

Amazon guided to ~$200B in 2026 capex. Amazon + Anthropic added another $25B commitment in April 2026. Lumentum’s CEO has framed hyperscaler AI infrastructure spend around ~$700B for 2025.

The key point: AI capex is not just GPUs. The cluster has to be connected. More compute means more optical links, more 800G/1.6T demand, more pressure on supply chains. But market structure matters.

Supply-chain reporting suggests Nvidia’s incremental 800G optical module orders are still mainly captured by InnoLight and Eoptolink, with the rest split across Coherent, Lumentum, Broadcom, and other US vendors. So for names like $AAOI, the bull case is not “market leader tomorrow.”

It’s more specific: A smaller US-onshore alternative sitting inside a real bottleneck, where even modest share gains, reshoring preference, or customer diversification can matter if the optics cycle keeps expanding. The thesis is real.
The debate now is execution, share capture, margin durability, and whether the market is underpricing the smaller beneficiaries.

text:
Diversification across the AI stack is key for long-term gains—not financial advice, do your own research & consider risk tolerance.

Hypothetical $100k split:
- Semis 35% (heavy NVDA/TSM/AVGO/AMD)
- Power/Grid 20% (VRT/ETN)
- Photonics/Networking 15%
- Memory/Compute 10%
- Space/Defense/Robotics 10% each (TSLA heavy here)

Rebalance quarterly as the cycle rotates. What's your risk level?

text:
Gustavo is a great example of why the bull market is over.

We do not have a bullish market but one bullish sector. If you do not own memory, photonics or - less likely for FinX, defensive names

You are underperforming.

This is why following strength and spotting names like $SOI or $SILC early enough, just after they give their first confirmations but not so lat that they gave all the returns, is so important.

Stocks' Strength > Convictions.

All due respect to Gustavo who I do not even know, his post came to my feed so I thought I could use it, but I know many are in the same boat, while not sharing it.
